record_office_terminology.land_records: County Clerk (deeds, mortgages, liens, oil/gas leases -- one office per county; pre-statehood land in Indian Territory was held as tribal allotments under the Dawes Act)
record_office_terminology.probate: District Court (wills, estates, guardianships -- probate jurisdiction in Oklahoma is in the District Court, not a separate Probate Court; guardianship records for minor allottees are especially important)
record_office_terminology.marriage_licenses: County Clerk (marriage licenses from statehood 1907; territorial-era marriages recorded by various authorities)
record_office_terminology.vital_records_state_start: 1908 (statewide birth and death registration; compliance improved after 1917; earlier vital events found in tribal records, church records, cemetery records, and newspapers)

burned_county_authority: Oklahoma Historical Society (OHS), Oklahoma City -- Oklahoma has several counties with record losses from courthouse fires, explosions, and natural disasters. OHS holds Indian Territory court records, territorial government records, tribal records, and microfilmed county records that supplement destroyed originals. NARA Fort Worth holds Bureau of Indian Affairs records, Dawes Commission records, and federal court records. BLM GLO land patents provide backup for Oklahoma Territory land records.
administrative_notes: Oklahoma was formed from the merger of two separate territories at statehood on November 16, 1907 (46th state). Indian Territory (eastern Oklahoma) was home to the Five Civilized Tribes (Cherokee, Chickasaw, Choctaw, Creek/Muscogee, Seminole) after their forced removal from the southeastern United States in the 1830s (Trail of Tears). Oklahoma Territory (western Oklahoma) was created in 1890 and settled through a series of land runs (1889, 1891, 1893), lotteries (1901), and sealed bids. The Dawes Commission (1893-1914) enrolled tribal members and allotted tribal lands to individuals, creating the Dawes Rolls -- the single most important genealogical resource for Oklahoma Native American research. Freedmen (formerly enslaved persons of the Five Tribes) were also enrolled and allotted land. Oklahoma had over 50 All-Black towns, more than any other state. The Osage Nation maintained unique mineral headrights. The oil boom transformed Tulsa, Oklahoma City, and Osage County. The Tulsa Race Massacre (1921) devastated the Greenwood District. Record offices: County Clerk (land, marriages), District Court (probate, guardianship). The Oklahoma Historical Society in Oklahoma City is the central repository. NARA Fort Worth holds extensive federal records for both territories.

core_repositories.description: Central repository for Oklahoma genealogical research. Holds Indian Territory records, Oklahoma Territory records, tribal court records, territorial government records, microfilmed county records, newspaper collections (Oklahoma Digital Newspaper Program), manuscript collections, maps, and photographs. The OHS Research Center provides access to county records, tribal records, and the Dawes Rolls. Essential for both Indian Territory and Oklahoma Territory research.

core_repositories.source_name: NARA -- National Archives at Fort Worth
core_repositories.repository: National Archives at Fort Worth, TX
core_repositories.description: Federal records for Oklahoma including Bureau of Indian Affairs records, Dawes Commission records (enrollment applications, census cards, allotment records), Union Agency records (Five Civilized Tribes), tribal court records, federal court records (Indian Territory courts), Oklahoma Territory land office records (homestead applications, final proofs), and military records (Fort Sill, Fort Reno, Fort Gibson).

core_repositories.source_name: Oklahoma Department of Libraries -- Genealogy Division
core_repositories.repository: Oklahoma Department of Libraries, Oklahoma City
core_repositories.description: State library with genealogical collections including published county histories, family histories, Oklahoma vital records indexes, and reference materials. Provides interlibrary loan services and maintains the Oklahoma Almanac.

familysearch_collections.description: Indexed and browsable collections including Oklahoma county records (deeds, probate, marriages), vital records, church records, and Dawes Rolls. FamilySearch microfilmed extensively at OHS, county offices, and tribal headquarters. Key collections include Oklahoma marriages, probate records, and Five Civilized Tribes enrollment cards.

Comprehensive research guide with county-specific pages, record availability guides, and links to digitized microfilm. Covers Oklahoma's unique dual-territory history, tribal records, Dawes Rolls, and All-Black town research.

newspapers_and_periodicals.source_name: Oklahoma Digital Newspaper Program (ODNP) -- The Gateway
newspapers_and_periodicals.repository: Oklahoma Historical Society / Oklahoma Department of Libraries
newspapers_and_periodicals.description: Digitized historic Oklahoma newspapers from the territorial period forward. The Gateway portal provides free access to Oklahoma newspapers. OHS holds the largest collection of Oklahoma newspapers on microfilm, including Indian Territory, Oklahoma Territory, and state papers. Legal notices, obituaries, land claim proofs, and community news.

Digitized historic Oklahoma newspapers including territorial papers. Oklahoma had an extensive newspaper tradition with papers in nearly every county seat and many small towns. Essential for legal notices, obituaries, and community records.

enslaved_persons_and_african_american.source_name: Oklahoma Freedmen and All-Black Town Records -- OHS / NARA
enslaved_persons_and_african_american.repository: Oklahoma Historical Society / NARA Fort Worth / Tribal headquarters
enslaved_persons_and_african_american.description: Oklahoma has a unique African American history centered on the Five Civilized Tribes' freedmen (formerly enslaved persons of the Cherokee, Chickasaw, Choctaw, Creek, and Seminole Nations). Freedmen were enrolled on the Dawes Rolls and allotted land. Freedmen rolls at NARA Fort Worth include family information and enrollment applications. Oklahoma had over 50 All-Black towns (Boley, Langston, Taft, Clearview, Rentiesville, etc.), more than any other state. The 1921 Tulsa Race Massacre destroyed the Greenwood District ('Black Wall Street'). OHS holds All-Black town records, African American newspapers, and community records. The Greenwood Cultural Center in Tulsa documents the massacre.

native_american_and_removal_records.source_name: Dawes Rolls and Five Civilized Tribes Records -- NARA / OHS
native_american_and_removal_records.repository: NARA Fort Worth / Oklahoma Historical Society / Tribal headquarters
native_american_and_removal_records.description: The Dawes Rolls (1898-1914) enrolled members of the Five Civilized Tribes (Cherokee, Chickasaw, Choctaw, Creek, Seminole) for individual land allotment. Enrollment categories include by Blood, Freedmen, Intermarried Whites, and (for Choctaw/Chickasaw) Mississippi Choctaw/Chickasaw. Application jackets contain detailed family information, birth/death dates, relationships, and testimony. Census cards group families. The Dawes Rolls are the foundation of tribal citizenship for the Five Tribes. NARA Fort Worth holds original records; OHS has microfilm copies; online access through NARA's Access to Archival Databases (AAD) and FamilySearch.

native_american_and_removal_records.source_name: Oklahoma Tribal Agency Records -- NARA Fort Worth
native_american_and_removal_records.repository: National Archives at Fort Worth, TX
native_american_and_removal_records.description: Bureau of Indian Affairs agency records for Oklahoma tribes: Union Agency (Five Civilized Tribes, Muskogee), Kiowa Agency (Anadarko), Cheyenne-Arapaho Agency (Concho/El Reno), Osage Agency (Pawhuska), Quapaw Agency (Miami), Pawnee Agency, Ponca Agency, Sac and Fox Agency, and others. Records include annuity rolls, allotment records, correspondence, agency census rolls, school records (Indian boarding schools), and land transaction records. Essential for tracing Native American families across all Oklahoma tribes.

immigration_and_naturalization.source_name: Oklahoma Naturalization and Immigration Records -- District Courts / NARA
immigration_and_naturalization.repository: County District Courts / NARA Fort Worth
immigration_and_naturalization.description: Naturalization records for Oklahoma immigrants are primarily in County District Court records. Coal mining counties (Pittsburg, Latimer, Le Flore, Coal, Haskell) attracted Italian, Welsh, Lithuanian, Slovak, and other European immigrants. Oklahoma Panhandle counties attracted later Mexican and Central American agricultural workers. Oil industry brought diverse workers. Federal court naturalization records at NARA Fort Worth. Oklahoma did not have major ports of entry, so immigrants typically entered through Galveston, New Orleans, or eastern ports.

military_records.source_name: Oklahoma Military Records -- OHS / NARA / Fort Sill / Fort Gibson
military_records.repository: Oklahoma Historical Society / NARA / Fort Sill / Fort Gibson
military_records.description: Civil War: Five Civilized Tribes split -- Cherokee Stand Watie's brigade (Confederate), Creek and Seminole factions on both sides; Indian Territory battles (Honey Springs 1863). Confederate and Union Indian service records at NARA. Post-Civil War: Fort Sill (1869-present), Fort Reno (1874-1908), Fort Gibson (1824-1890), Fort Supply (1868-1894) records at NARA Fort Worth. Spanish-American War, WWI, WWII service records. Oklahoma adjutant general records at OHS. The 45th Infantry Division (Oklahoma National Guard) Museum in Oklahoma City has WWII records.

military_records.source_name: Civil War Soldiers and Sailors System -- Oklahoma / Indian Territory
military_records.description: Searchable database of Civil War service records for Indian Territory soldiers on both Union and Confederate sides. Five Civilized Tribes contributed regiments to both armies. Cherokee, Creek, and Seminole forces were divided; Choctaw and Chickasaw primarily supported the Confederacy.

land_and_property.source_name: BLM General Land Office Records -- Oklahoma
land_and_property.repository: Bureau of Land Management, General Land Office
land_and_property.description: Oklahoma Territory (western Oklahoma) is a public land state. Federal land patents (homestead, cash entry) document original land claims from the 1889 Land Run, 1891 openings, 1893 Cherokee Outlet run, 1901 KCA lottery, and other openings. BLM GLO website provides free online access to digitized patents and survey plats. Eastern Oklahoma land was distributed through tribal allotment (Dawes Act), not GLO patents.

Each Oklahoma county has a County Clerk office maintaining land records (deeds, mortgages, liens, oil/gas leases). Records begin from county organization (1889-1912 depending on the county). Oil and gas lease records are a unique Oklahoma feature. OHS and FamilySearch have microfilmed deed books for some counties.

vital_records.repository: Oklahoma State Department of Health (OSDH), Oklahoma City / OHS
vital_records.description: Statewide birth and death registration began in 1908 but compliance was poor until about 1917. OSDH maintains vital records. OHS holds historical vital records indexes. Marriage records are held by County Clerks (from statehood 1907; some territorial-era marriages). For pre-registration vital events, researchers must use tribal records, church records, cemetery records, and newspaper notices.

church_and_cemetery.source_name: Oklahoma Church Records -- OHS / Denominational Archives
church_and_cemetery.repository: Oklahoma Historical Society / Local congregations / Denominational archives
church_and_cemetery.description: Church records are important for Oklahoma genealogy, especially for pre-statehood Indian Territory. Missionary records (Presbyterian, Methodist, Baptist missions to Five Tribes) document tribal families. Catholic diocesan archives (Oklahoma City, Tulsa) hold parish records. Mennonite church records for north-central Oklahoma. African Methodist Episcopal church records for freedmen communities. Oklahoma Indian Mission Conference (Methodist) records are at OHS.

Crowdsourced cemetery transcriptions for Oklahoma. Oklahoma has diverse cemetery types: tribal cemeteries, All-Black town cemeteries, mining community cemeteries, and pioneer burial grounds. Coverage is growing. The Oklahoma Genealogical Society and county societies have published transcription volumes.

research_guides_and_societies.description: Statewide genealogical society publishing the Oklahoma Genealogical Society Quarterly. Maintains surname files, Bible record collections, and research guides. Hosts annual conferences. Library at the Oklahoma City Metropolitan Library System.
Volunteer-contributed county-level genealogy resources for all 77 Oklahoma counties. Includes cemetery transcriptions, Bible records, query boards, and research guides.

Museum and research center documenting the history and culture of the Cherokee, Chickasaw, Choctaw, Creek (Muscogee), and Seminole Nations. Historical materials, artwork, and cultural artifacts. Genealogical research assistance for tribal families.

historical_counties_extinct.description: Oklahoma had several extinct or renamed counties during the territorial period. Day County (Oklahoma Territory, 1891-1907) was absorbed into Ellis, Dewey, and Roger Mills counties at statehood. Greer County was originally organized as a Texas county (1886) before the US Supreme Court assigned it to Oklahoma Territory in 1896. Several recording districts in Indian Territory (Tobucksy, Skullyville, Moshulatubbee, etc.) were administrative subdivisions of the Five Tribes that became counties at statehood. Oklahoma Territory originally had seven counties (Beaver, Canadian, Cleveland, Kingfisher, Logan, Oklahoma, Payne) from the 1889 Land Run, expanded as new lands were opened. Researchers should check under both territorial recording district names and modern county names at OHS and NARA.

burned_counties_summary.notes: Oklahoma has 5 counties with significant record losses. Carter County suffered from an 1895 railroad explosion in Ardmore and a 1915 courthouse fire. Dewey County's 1916 courthouse fire destroyed most early records. Haskell County's 1916 courthouse fire destroyed many early records. Lincoln County experienced an 1895 courthouse fire and 1897 tornado damage. Okfuskee County's 1907 courthouse fire (shortly after statehood) destroyed initial records. For Indian Territory counties, tribal allotment records and Dawes Commission records at NARA Fort Worth provide critical backup. For Oklahoma Territory counties, BLM GLO federal land patents supplement destroyed county deed books. OHS in Oklahoma City holds microfilmed county records and territorial records.

burned_jurisdictions.record_losses: 1895 explosion and fire in Ardmore; 1915 courthouse fire
Courthouse fire 1916 destroyed most early records
Courthouse fire 1916 destroyed many early records
